title: Three operators, same wire, three amounts

scripts.subtitle: Classic PAS opener
scripts.original_vo: Listen. Three business owners. Same day. Same Chinese supplier. Same 50K wire. Three different amounts arrived. Here's the deal. Business owner A used a major bank. Supplier got 49,250. Business owner B used a regional bank. Supplier got 49,700. Business owner C used a specialist provider. Supplier got 49,850. Same wire. 600 dollar spread. And all three of them paid the same line item. No fee. Stop. The wire fee is not the cost. The exchange rate is the cost. Banks hide a 1.5% margin in the rate so you never see the line. On 50K that's 750 dollars. Run that across 12 wires a year. You just gave your bank 9,000 dollars for nothing. Period. WorldFirst sits at 0.3% margin. Same wire. Supplier gets the full amount. Same day to Chinese accounts. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Here is something most business owners in Southeast Asia have never been told. Three business owners sent the same wire on the same day to the same Chinese supplier. Each one sent 50,000 US dollars. But the supplier got three different amounts. Let us walk through it. Business owner A used a major bank. The supplier got 49,250. Business owner B used a regional bank. The supplier got 49,700. Business owner C used a specialist payment provider. The supplier got 49,850. Same wire. Same day. Same supplier. A 600 dollar gap between the best and the worst. Now here is the part that catches people out. All three of them looked at their receipts and saw the same thing. Wire fee: zero. So all three thought the wire was free. It was not. The wire fee is not the real cost. The exchange rate is the real cost. FX, short for foreign exchange, is the rate your bank uses to convert your money into Chinese yuan. Your bank quietly adds a margin of about 1.5 percent into that rate. You never see it on a line item. On a 50,000 dollar wire, that 1.5 percent is 750 dollars. Now imagine you do 12 wires a year. That is 9,000 dollars going to your bank every year, just from the hidden rate margin. We do this differently at WorldFirst. We sit closer to 0.3 percent. Same wire. The supplier gets close to the full amount. Same day to Chinese bank accounts. Search WorldFirst.

The first got 49,250. The second got 49,700. The third got 49,850. That is a 600 dollar gap on a wire that should have been identical. Now let us stretch that out over a year. Say you send 12 wires a year. Each one is 50,000 dollars. That is 600,000 dollars a year going to your Chinese suppliers. A salary for a staff member. A full container of inventory. The next product line you have been waiting to launch. And here is the punchline. It is not a fee. There is no line item to point at. It is the exchange rate margin baked into your wire. Most banks add 1 to 2 percent into the rate so you never see it on paper. We sit closer to 0.3 percent. Same wires. Same day to Chinese bank accounts. Search WorldFirst.

scripts.subtitle: Mechanism-first reveal
scripts.original_vo: Here's the problem most business owners don't realize. Two wires to the same supplier on the same day can land at different amounts. Why? The exchange rate. Your bank picks the rate. They don't show you what they picked relative to the real market rate. They just show you the wire fee. Zero. Waived. Free. So you think the wire was free. Three business owners proved this last week. All sent 50K to the same Chinese factory. The bank customer's supplier got 49,250. The regional bank got 49,700. The specialist got 49,850. 600 dollar spread on identical wires. Same line item. Different reality. Stop assuming the fee line tells you the cost. The rate is the cost. WorldFirst sits at 0.3% margin on USD to CNY. Same day. Full amount lands. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Here is the part most business owners do not realise. Two wires to the same supplier on the same day can land at different amounts. Why? Because of the exchange rate. Let us break it down. When you wire dollars to China, your bank has to swap your dollars into Chinese yuan, or CNY. Your bank picks the rate they use for that swap. They do not show you what they picked compared to the real market rate. They just show you the wire fee on the receipt. Zero. Waived. Free. So you think the wire was free. It was not. Three business owners proved this last week. All three sent 50,000 US dollars to the same Chinese factory on the same day. The one who used the major bank, their supplier received 49,250. The one who used a regional bank, supplier got 49,700. The one who used a specialist provider, supplier got 49,850. A 600 dollar spread on three identical wires. Same line item on the receipt. Three different realities on the supplier side. The fee line is not the cost. The rate is the cost. We sit closer to 0.3 percent rate margin on US dollar to Chinese yuan. Same day to Chinese accounts. Search WorldFirst.

scripts.subtitle: Truth nobody's telling you / conspiratorial
scripts.original_vo: Here's the truth nobody's telling you about international wires. Three business owners sent 50K each to the same Chinese supplier yesterday. Three different amounts arrived. 49,250. 49,700. 49,850. Why? Banks don't make their money on the wire fee. They make it on the rate. They pick a rate. You can't see what they picked. You can't compare. You just see the receipt that says no fee, wire complete. Meanwhile they pocketed 1 to 2% of the wire in the rate margin. Silently. Every single wire. WorldFirst doesn't do that. 0.3% margin. The rate you see is close to the real rate. Same day to Chinese accounts. That's the difference. Period.

Three different amounts arrived on the other side. The first got 49,250. The second got 49,700. The third got 49,850. Why does that happen? Because banks do not earn most of their money on the wire fee. They earn it on the rate. Here is how. When you wire dollars to China, the bank has to convert your dollars into Chinese yuan. The bank picks the exchange rate they use. You cannot see what rate they picked compared to the real market rate. You cannot easily compare. You just see the receipt that says no fee, wire complete. Meanwhile, behind that simple receipt, the bank took 1 to 2 percent of the wire as their rate margin. Quietly. Every single wire. We are built differently at WorldFirst. Our margin sits closer to 0.3 percent. The rate you see is close to the real market rate. Same day to Chinese accounts. That is the difference. Search WorldFirst.

scripts.subtitle: Real talk + escalating personal energy
scripts.original_vo: Real talk for every business owner paying Chinese suppliers. You think you and your friend are getting the same rate? You're not. Three business owners. Same 50K wire. Same supplier. Same day. Three different amounts. 49,250. 49,700. 49,850. The one who got the worst rate is paying for the bank's margin and doesn't even know it. The receipt says no fee. The wire went through. Everyone's happy on paper. Meanwhile you're 600 dollars down on one wire. 7,200 down on 12 wires a year. I see this every single week. Every week. Stop. WorldFirst. 0.3% rate margin. Same day to Chinese bank accounts. The supplier gets the full amount. Your margin stays your margin. Search WorldFirst. Now.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: For every business owner paying Chinese suppliers, here is something to think about. You think you and your friend are getting the same rate? You probably are not. Three business owners ran the same wire. Each one sent 50,000 US dollars. Same supplier. Same day. Three different amounts landed on the other side. One got 49,250. One got 49,700. One got 49,850. The one who got the worst rate is paying for the bank's hidden margin and does not even know it. The receipt says no fee. The wire went through fine. Everyone looks happy on paper. Meanwhile that one business owner is 600 dollars down on one wire. Over 12 wires in a year, that is 7,200 dollars down. This happens every single week across Southeast Asia. Every week. We are WorldFirst. Around 0.3 percent rate margin. Same day to Chinese bank accounts. The supplier gets close to the full amount. Your margin stays in your business, not the bank's. Search WorldFirst.

PH1.analysis: The three-supplier comparison anchored in the findings (49,250 / 49,700 / 49,850 on a 50K wire) is defensible because it's the card's own evidence. '1.5% margin = 750 on 50K' aligns with the findings range of 0.6 to 1.5% across providers. The '12 wires a year' compound is a generic average, not a verified scenario, and the WF '0.3% margin' / 'full amount lands' framing is a typical-case figure presented as universal. CN-side receiving bank fees can still apply and are not addressed.

skeptic_comments.text: Where this 1.5% number from? My RHB never break it down like this.
12 wires a year? I send 3 a year only, this math don't apply to me.
WorldFirst is what? Sound like crypto thing. Got license in Malaysia or not?
If three guys really same wire same day, who showed you the receipts? Where's the proof.
Bank still better lah, at least I can walk in branch when problem.

PH2.analysis: Compound math from 600 spread to 7,200 a year assumes consistent margin on every wire of the year, which is plausible but not guaranteed (rates fluctuate, some wires hit better correspondent paths). '21,600 over 3 years' is illustrative and frames it as a typical case. The supplier-end amounts are grounded in the card's findings but the 'employee / inventory / product line' translation is rhetorical. WF '0.3%' figure should ideally say 'around 0.3%' since it varies by pair.

skeptic_comments.text: Compound math sound nice but rate change every day, where got always 1.5%.
21,600 over 3 years is best case for the seller side of this story.
If WorldFirst really 0.3 percent, why bank can't match? Got hidden somewhere.
I pay 50K once a year not 12 times, this not apply to small shop like me.
Container of inventory? Got receipt no? Show la.

PH3.analysis: 'Your bank stole / quietly took' is loaded language even after the iteration soften. Banks legally disclose FX margins in T&Cs, so 'stole' is contestable. The 600 dollar number and the three-band comparison are grounded in the findings. The 'no receipt, no notification, no refund' framing is accurate for most bank wire confirmations but stated absolutely. WF '0.3% margin' is presented as a static fact rather than a typical-case range.

skeptic_comments.text: Stole is heavy word la, my Maybank statement got rate clearly stated.
If bank really stealing, where's the regulator? They got license one.
Quietly took also got the rate on the receipt, you just never look.
WorldFirst is which country company? Got MAS license or not?
600 dollar on 50K wire is 1.2%, not 600 dollar on every wire la.

PH4.analysis: This variant is the most defensible. Mechanism reveal (rate not fee is the cost) is fundamentally accurate. The three-amount comparison is the card's own evidence. The iterated VO removes 'Stop' and 'Period' and downgrades absolutist phrasing. 'Closer to 0.3% margin' is a more honest framing of WF's rate. Only soft flag is 'two wires same day can land at different amounts' is true but the three-business comparison is illustrative, not a documented case study.

skeptic_comments.text: Okay this make sense, but is this an actual story or made up example?
If rate is the real cost, where I see the real rate to compare?
0.3% on USD to CNY, who confirm? Got screenshot?
Three business owner case study very convenient ah.
WorldFirst is who? Never heard, then need verify lor.

PH5.analysis: Problem stack is well-grounded (three identical wires, three results, all receipts say zero fee). 'Wire fees are not the full cost' is accurate. 'Your bank earns through the rate margin you don't see' is true. WF 'closer to 0.3% margin' is honest. The 'Same wire, close to the full amount lands' line is iterated to acknowledge minor receiving-side variance. Mainly a structural script, low factual risk but compresses three real cases as a single narrative without sourcing.

skeptic_comments.text: All three got zero fee on receipt but rate was different, where do I see the rate then?
Where these three business owners from? VN? MY? SG? Pair matters.
Close to 0.3% means what, 0.5 or 0.3? Just say the number.
If you ship from China this is worth knowing, but I source from Vietnam, so irrelevant.
WorldFirst what license they got in my country?

PG1.analysis: The three-amount comparison is grounded in the card's findings. '1 to 2% margin' aligns with the findings (0.6 to 1.5%). 'The supplier gets close to the full amount' is appropriately hedged. The 600-dollar gap claim is presented as a typical scenario, which is fine. WF '0.3% margin' kept as 'closer to 0.3%' is honest. Generally defensible but the three-business setup is illustrative, not a documented case file.

skeptic_comments.text: Sent at same hour same supplier different amount, this happen in real life or just example?
1 to 2% in the rate, my bank statement got the rate written, you just dramatising.
Closer to 0.3, why no exact number?
Friend got 600 more than me, that's me being slow, not bank stealing.
Never hear WorldFirst before, sound like overseas thing. Got Malaysia office?

PG2.analysis: Compound math (600 x 12 = 7,200) is arithmetically clean but assumes 12 wires a year per business owner, which is a typical mid-size importer scenario not all SEA SMEs. 'Add two more factories you're at 20K' is rhetorical and presented as universal. The factual core (bank rate margin, supplier-end gap, WF closer to 0.3%) is grounded. The 'employee you didn't hire' line is rhetorical translation, fine for the format.

skeptic_comments.text: 12 wires a year is for big shop, I do 4 a year. Maths different.
If WorldFirst really 0.3 then everyone should switch why nobody talking about it?
20K a year employee, in which country bro. Not in Singapore.
Where does the rate margin number come from, source pls.
Same supplier same day three different amount, very dramatic. Got proof?

PG3.analysis: The 'wrong tool' framing is brand-safe and defensible. The three-band comparison is grounded. 'Cross-border CNY is not its specialty' is accurate for most consumer-grade SEA banks. WF 'around 0.3% rate margin' is honestly hedged. The screwdriver analogy is rhetorical. Soft flag is 'built for this exact wire' overstates WF as a single purpose-built tool when it's a broader multi-currency service.

skeptic_comments.text: Rethink using bank, then what about local payment? Still need bank what.
Maybank do CNY wire fine la, where got built only for domestic.
Built for this exact wire sound like marketing line.
0.3% why need 'around', either is or isn't.
WorldFirst safe ah? Got insurance like bank deposit?

PG4.analysis: The mechanism explanation (banks earn through rate margin, not wire fee) is accurate. The three-amount comparison is grounded. 'Quietly took 1 to 2%' is hedged from 'pocketed' and aligns with findings. WF 'around 0.3%' is honest. The conspiratorial frame is softened in the iteration. Soft flag is the implication that banks deliberately hide the rate. They disclose it, just not next to a mid-market reference.

skeptic_comments.text: Banks earn through rate margin, true, but they also got compliance cost. Not pure profit.
Most people don't realise this, but it's literally on the wire confirmation.
Quietly took, the rate is on the receipt bro just compare it.
Three different amount on same wire same supplier same day, source?
Built differently, like every fintech say. Need MAS or BNM license la.

PG5.analysis: Personal-energy variant. The three-band comparison is grounded. 'You and your friend not getting the same rate' is true for most SEA importers using different banks. '600 down on one wire, 7,200 down on 12 wires' is arithmetic from the typical case. WF 'around 0.3%' is hedged honestly. The 'I see this every week' line is rhetorical, not from a verified dataset. Mainly a delivery-style risk, not factual.

skeptic_comments.text: This happen every week, where you see it. You work at bank or what.
Friend pay different rate than me, sure, but it's the friend's bank not stealing from me la.
7,200 a year on 12 wires assumes I'm doing 12 wires. I do 5.
Around 0.3, sounds vague.
WorldFirst is from where, who own them?
